questions.stem: A group $G$ acts on a set $X$ if there is a map $G \times X \to X$, $(g,x) \mapsto g \cdot x$, satisfying which two conditions?
options: $e \cdot x = x$ and $g \cdot (h \cdot x) = (gh) \cdot x$ for all $g, h \in G$, $x \in X$.
$g \cdot x = x$ for all $g$, and the map is a bijection.
$g \cdot (h \cdot x) = (hg) \cdot x$ and $g \cdot x = g$ for all $g$.
$e \cdot x = e$ and $g \cdot (h \cdot x) = h \cdot (g \cdot x)$.
questions.explanation: An action must fix every point under the identity, $e \cdot x = x$, and be compatible with the group product, $g \cdot (h \cdot x) = (gh) \cdot x$. These two axioms are exactly what make the action a homomorphism into the bijections of $X$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a fixed point $x \in X$, the orbit $\mathrm{Orb}(x)$ of the action of $G$ on $X$ is which set?
options: $\{x \in X : g \cdot x = x \text{ for all } g\}$.
The set of all bijections of $X$.
questions.explanation: The orbit of $x$ is the set of all images $\{g \cdot x : g \in G\}$ of $x$ under the group, a subset of $X$. The set $\{g \in G : g \cdot x = x\}$ is instead the stabilizer.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a point $x \in X$, the stabilizer $\mathrm{Stab}(x)$ of the action of $G$ on $X$ is which object?
options: A subset of $X$ that need not be a subgroup.
The whole group $G$ in every case.
The subgroup $\{g \in G : g \cdot x = x\}$ of $G$.
questions.explanation: The stabilizer $\{g \in G : g \cdot x = x\}$ collects the group elements that fix $x$. It is always a subgroup of $G$, since it contains $e$ and is closed under products and inverses.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The Orbit-Stabilizer theorem states that, for a group $G$ acting on $X$ and any $x \in X$, there is a bijection between which two sets?
questions.explanation: The map $g\,\mathrm{Stab}(x) \mapsto g \cdot x$ is a bijection from the coset space $G/\mathrm{Stab}(x)$ onto the orbit $\mathrm{Orb}(x)$. For finite $G$ this gives $|G| = |\mathrm{Stab}(x)|\,|\mathrm{Orb}(x)|$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a finite group $G$ acting on $X$, the Orbit-Stabilizer theorem gives which counting formula?
questions.explanation: Since $G/\mathrm{Stab}(x)$ is in bijection with the orbit, the number of cosets times $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|$ equals $|G|$. Hence $|G| = |\mathrm{Stab}(x)|\,|\mathrm{Orb}(x)|$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An action of $G$ on $X$ corresponds to a group homomorphism $\theta$ into which group?
options: $\mathrm{Bij}(X)$, the group of all bijections of $X$ under composition.
questions.explanation: Each $g$ gives a bijection $\theta(g): x \mapsto g \cdot x$, and the action axioms make $\theta : G \to \mathrm{Bij}(X)$ a homomorphism. Conversely any such homomorphism defines an action.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An action of $G$ on $X$ is called faithful (or effective) when which condition holds?
options: Every orbit is a single point.
Every stabilizer is the whole group $G$.
questions.explanation: Faithfulness means $\theta : G \to \mathrm{Bij}(X)$ is injective, so distinct group elements act as distinct bijections. Equivalently, only the identity fixes every point of $X$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A fundamental set $F$ for an action of $G$ on $X$ is a subset that contains how many elements from each orbit?
options: At least two elements from each orbit.
Exactly one element from each orbit.
questions.explanation: A fundamental set picks exactly one representative from each orbit. The quotient $X/G$ is then in bijection with $F$, so $F$ is a model for the set of orbits.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The relation $x \sim y \iff y = g \cdot x$ for some $g \in G$ on the set $X$ is which kind of relation?
options: An equivalence relation whose classes are the orbits.
A relation that is reflexive but never symmetric.
A total order whose classes are the stabilizers.
questions.explanation: Being in the same orbit is reflexive (take $g = e$), symmetric (use $g^{-1}$), and transitive (compose), so it is an equivalence relation. Its equivalence classes are exactly the orbits, and $X/G$ is the set of classes.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The group $\mathrm{GL}(n, \mathbb{R})$ of invertible real $n \times n$ matrices acts naturally on $\mathbb{R}^n$ by which rule?
questions.explanation: The standard linear action sends $(M, x)$ to $Mx$. The identity matrix fixes every vector and $(MN)x = M(Nx)$, so the two action axioms hold.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Let $\mathbb{Z}$ act on $\mathbb{R}^2$ by translation $n \cdot x = x + n\mathbf{i}$, where $\mathbf{i}$ is the unit horizontal vector. What is the stabilizer of a point $x$?
questions.explanation: Translation by $n\mathbf{i}$ fixes $x$ only when $n\mathbf{i} = 0$, that is $n = 0$. So the stabilizer of every point is trivial, while the orbit is the doubly infinite set $\{x + n\mathbf{i} : n \in \mathbb{Z}\}$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: With $\mathbb{Z}$ acting on $\mathbb{R}^2$ by $n \cdot x = x + n\mathbf{i}$, the quotient $\mathbb{R}^2 / \mathbb{Z}$ can be identified with which surface?
questions.explanation: The strip $\{0 \le x_1 < 1\}$ is a fundamental set, and identifying its two opposite edges produces a cylinder. So $\mathbb{R}^2/\mathbb{Z}$ is a cylinder.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: When the group $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ acts on $\mathbb{R}^2$ by $(n_1, n_2) \cdot x = x + (n_1, n_2)$, the quotient $\mathbb{R}^2 / (\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z})$ is which surface?
questions.explanation: The unit square $\{0 \le x_1 < 1, \ 0 \le x_2 < 1\}$ is a fundamental set. Identifying both pairs of opposite edges glues the square into a torus, so the quotient is a torus.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The group $\mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{C})$ acts on the Riemann sphere $\mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}$ by which formula?
options: $\left(\begin{smallmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{smallmatrix}\right) \cdot z = az + b$.
$\left(\begin{smallmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{smallmatrix}\right) \cdot z = \dfrac{az + b}{cz + d}$.
$\left(\begin{smallmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{smallmatrix}\right) \cdot z = \dfrac{cz + d}{az + b}$.
$\left(\begin{smallmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{smallmatrix}\right) \cdot z = z + (a + d)$.
questions.explanation: The matrix acts as the Mobius transformation $z \mapsto (az + b)/(cz + d)$. This is the fundamental example of a group acting on a geometric space that runs through the course.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A group $G$ acts on $X$. The number of distinct cosets of $\mathrm{Stab}(x)$ in $G$ equals which quantity?
questions.explanation: The Orbit-Stabilizer bijection sends cosets of $\mathrm{Stab}(x)$ to orbit points. So the index $[G : \mathrm{Stab}(x)]$, the number of cosets, equals the orbit size $|\mathrm{Orb}(x)|$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A finite group $G$ of order $24$ acts on a set, and one point $x$ has an orbit of size $6$. What is the order of $\mathrm{Stab}(x)$?
questions.explanation: By Orbit-Stabilizer, $|G| = |\mathrm{Stab}(x)|\,|\mathrm{Orb}(x)|$, so $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = 24/6 = 4$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A finite group of order $60$ acts on a set so that the stabilizer of a point $x$ has order $5$. What is the size of the orbit of $x$?
questions.explanation: From $|\mathrm{Orb}(x)| = |G| / |\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = 60/5 = 12$. The orbit therefore contains $12$ points.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which statement about orbits of an action of $G$ on $X$ is correct?
options: Two orbits can overlap in part without being equal.
The orbits partition $X$ into disjoint subsets.
Every orbit has the same number of elements as $G$.
There is always exactly one orbit.
questions.explanation: Orbits are the equivalence classes of the relation $x \sim y$ when $y = g \cdot x$, so they partition $X$: distinct orbits are disjoint and their union is all of $X$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An action of $G$ on $X$ is called transitive when which condition holds?
options: There is only one orbit, namely all of $X$.
Every point is fixed by every group element.
questions.explanation: Transitivity means any point can be carried to any other, so the whole of $X$ is a single orbit. For the regularity of the Platonic solids one uses that the symmetry group acts transitively on the vertices.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: If a group $G$ acts on $X$ and $g \cdot x = h \cdot x$ for $g, h \in G$, which statement must hold?
options: $x$ is a fixed point of the whole group.
questions.explanation: From $g \cdot x = h \cdot x$ we get $(h^{-1}g) \cdot x = x$, so $h^{-1}g \in \mathrm{Stab}(x)$, that is $g$ lies in the coset $h\,\mathrm{Stab}(x)$. This is the calculation behind the Orbit-Stabilizer bijection.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Points $x$ and $y$ lie in the same orbit of an action of $G$ on $X$. How are their stabilizers related?
options: They have no relation in general.
questions.explanation: If $y = g \cdot x$ then $\mathrm{Stab}(y) = g\,\mathrm{Stab}(x)\,g^{-1}$, so stabilizers of points in one orbit are conjugate. In particular they all have the same order, consistent with Orbit-Stabilizer.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A group $G$ of order $48$ acts on a set with exactly two orbits, of sizes $6$ and $a$, where the stabilizer of a point in the second orbit has order $16$. What is $a$?
questions.explanation: For the second orbit, $a = |G| / |\mathrm{Stab}| = 48/16 = 3$. The orbit sizes $6$ and $3$ each divide $|G| = 48$, as Orbit-Stabilizer requires.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A finite group $G$ of order $36$ acts on a set $X$. A point $x$ has an orbit of size $9$. Give the order of $\mathrm{Stab}(x)$.
questions.solution: By the Orbit-Stabilizer theorem $|G| = |\mathrm{Stab}(x)|\,|\mathrm{Orb}(x)|$, so $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = 36/9$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = |G|/|\mathrm{Orb}(x)| = 36/9 = 4$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A group $G$ of order $120$ acts on a set, and the stabilizer of a point $x$ has order $10$. Give the size of the orbit of $x$.
questions.solution: The orbit size is $|G|/|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = 120/10$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: $|\mathrm{Orb}(x)| = |G|/|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = 120/10 = 12$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The group $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ acts on $\mathbb{R}^2$ by integer translations. A fundamental set is the unit square. State whether the quotient $\mathbb{R}^2/(\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z})$ is a torus.
questions.solution: Identifying both pairs of opposite edges of the unit square glues it into a surface. The quotient {{1}} a torus.
questions.explanation: The unit square with opposite edges identified is the standard construction of a torus, so the quotient is a torus.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A finite group $G$ of order $24$ acts on a set with two orbits. The first orbit has size $8$. The stabilizer of a point in the second orbit has order $12$. Give the size of the second orbit.
questions.solution: The second orbit has size $|G|/|\mathrm{Stab}| = 24/12$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: The second orbit size is $24/12 = 2$. Both orbit sizes, $8$ and $2$, divide $|G| = 24$ as required.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups
